howItWorks.intro: This power consumption calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
howItWorks.outro: Energy costs and incentives change by region and season. Check with your local utility for current rates.

faq.question: What is the difference between watts and kWh?
faq.answer: Watts measure power (rate of energy use). kWh measure energy (total consumption over time). A 100W bulb uses 100 watts of power. Running it for 10 hours uses 1 kWh of energy. You pay for kWh, not watts.

faq.question: How do I convert watts to kWh?
faq.answer: Multiply watts by hours, then divide by 1,000. Example: 1,500W heater for 3 hours = 1,500 × 3 ÷ 1,000 = 4.5 kWh. At $0.14/kWh, that costs $0.63.

faq.question: How many watts is 1 amp?
faq.answer: Watts = Amps × Volts. In the US (120V): 1 amp = 120 watts. A 15-amp circuit can handle 1,800 watts max (15A × 120V). A 20-amp circuit: 2,400 watts max.

faq.question: Is the Power Consumption Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard energy and utilities formulas trusted by homeowners and businesses. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

sections.title: What Is the Power Consumption Calculator?
sections.body: The Power Consumption Calculator is a free online tool designed for homeowners and businesses who need quick, accurate calculations in the energy and utilities space. By entering your power rating, hours of use, cost per kwh, you get instant results including energy used, cost, if used daily. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting energy used right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In energy and utilities, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by homeowners and businesses worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to manage energy costs with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your power rating and need to find the right energy used. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Hours of Use
howToSteps.description: Type or select your hours of use in the field provided. Use the most accurate value available for best results.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Cost per kWh ($)
howToSteps.description: Type or select your cost per kwh in the field provided. Use the most accurate value available for best results.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your energy used, cost, if used daily. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your power rating before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both energy used and cost — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to manage energy costs.
If you're unsure about your cost per kwh, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
